items.title: Strategy sets aside $3.8B after STRC crashed to $71, authorizes its first-ever buybacks
items.idea: After STRC (Strategy's Bitcoin-backed preferred) fell to $71.25 and Strive's SATA to $79 last week, Strategy unveiled a $3.8B Digital Credit Capital Framework: a $2.55B cash reserve dedicated to dividends/interest (board must approve dropping below 12 months of coverage) plus a new program to sell up to $1.25B of Bitcoin if needed -- under 2.5% of its 847,363 BTC. STRC's dividend rose from 11.5% to 12%, and Strategy authorized its first-ever buybacks ($1B preferred + $1B MSTR common). STRC/MSTR both rallied ~12% Monday.
items.angle: This is the first real stress test of 'Bitcoin treasury as capital markets machine' -- watch whether the optional BTC-sale lever stays a one-off backstop or becomes the start of a slow bleed.
items.tweetA: Saylor built an empire on 'never sell.' Now there's a $1.25B BTC sale option sitting on the balance sheet as a dividend backstop. That's not a top signal -- it's under 2.5% of the stack -- but it's the first crack in a decade of dogma. $MSTR $STRC
items.tweetB: The real story isn't the $3.8B liquidity buffer, it's that Saylor just authorized his first-ever stock buybacks. A guy who's spent 5 years telling you never to sell BTC just told you Strategy's own securities can be mispriced enough to buy back. Which one do you believe?

items.title: 65% of family offices expect the dollar to weaken, 56% fear a debt crisis within 5 years
items.idea: UBS's 2026 Global Family Office Report: 65% of family offices expect confidence in the dollar to weaken, 56% fear a debt crisis within five years -- their stated response is increasing hard-asset allocations. Global debt hit a record $348T at end-2025. The IMF separately warned stocks and bonds are increasingly falling together during stress, because governments keep flooding markets with new debt issuance.
items.angle: This is institutional money quietly admitting the debasement trade isn't a trade, it's the base case -- worth tracking family office flow data as a leading indicator for gold/BTC allocation shifts.
items.tweetA: 56% of family offices surveyed by UBS think there's a debt crisis coming within 5 years. $348T in global debt and counting. That's not a doomer newsletter talking, that's the people managing the world's largest pools of capital telling you what they're actually doing with it.
items.tweetB: Everyone still treats bonds as the stock-market hedge. IMF says that correlation broke -- stocks and bonds are now falling together in stress because governments won't stop issuing debt. If your 60/40 portfolio assumes 2015 correlations, you're already exposed.

items.title: An AI safety super PAC hid $2M in election spending until after the vote it influenced
items.idea: Public First Action (an AI-safety-aligned PAC network claiming $80M raised) routed $2M to Latino Victory Fund to back Colorado candidate Manny Rutinel, without disclosing the arrangement until after his primary win -- exploiting FEC reporting-timeline gaps. Campaign finance experts note the identical tactic was used weeks earlier by an AIPAC-aligned group in Illinois, which routed $5.3M through two PACs branded around women's issues and affordability.
items.angle: AI policy money is sophisticated enough to game campaign finance disclosure timing -- a preview of how the industry will fight state AI regulation battles nationwide as more state AI acts come up (see Colorado's AI Act, which Rutinel sponsored and which was later repealed).
items.tweetA: An AI safety PAC just hid $2M in election spending behind a Latino voter-turnout group until after the vote. Same trick AIPAC-aligned money used in Illinois weeks earlier. This isn't a bug in campaign finance law, it's becoming the standard playbook for tech money in 2026.
items.tweetB: Public First backed Rutinel, who sponsored Colorado's AI Act before industry got it repealed. Now the same network is laundering its spending through minority-outreach PACs. Whoever wins the AI regulation fight in 2026 will win it with better disclosure lawyers, not better arguments.

items.title: ADP misses (98K vs 120K) but the real event is Warsh sharing a stage with Lagarde at Sintra today
items.idea: June ADP printed 98K vs 120K expected -- a miss Bespoke calls too small to upend the 'improving labor market' theme. Meanwhile the 10-year yield jumped 7bp to 4.49% pre-market, crude sits below $70, and Fed Chair Kevin Warsh appears alongside ECB's Lagarde at Sintra later this morning for his first real market test since taking the chair.
items.angle: The ADP miss is noise; the Sintra appearance is the actual event -- a hawkish tone risks the 10Y testing 4.5%+, a dovish tone undercuts Warsh's 'not a sock puppet' positioning.
items.tweetA: ADP miss (98K vs 120K) barely moves the market. Warsh at Sintra today is the actual event. Either he confirms the hawkish read and the 10Y tests 4.5%, or he softens and immediately proves the 'sock puppet' critics right. There's no good answer for him.
items.tweetB: Why did 10Y yields jump 7bp on a jobs MISS this morning? Because markets have stopped trading data and started trading Warsh's mouth. That's a regime change in itself -- forward guidance is dead, personality risk is the new variable.

items.title: Small business added 53K jobs in June, but employment is still shrinking -0.28% year-on-year
items.idea: Barry Knapp's read on the ADP print: small businesses added 53,000 jobs, but the year-on-year growth rate of small-business employment is still -0.28% -- a marginal improvement from -0.30% in May, and still well off the -0.52% low from December 2025. His conclusion: monetary policy remains restrictive for small businesses even as the headline print looks fine.
items.angle: The equity-market bifurcation between Mag7 and small-cap semis has a labor-market mirror -- small business hiring is still contracting year-on-year while the aggregate print looks healthy.
items.tweetA: Small business employment is still shrinking -0.28% y/y even after a 'good' ADP month. Down from -0.52% in December, but still negative. If you're only reading today's 98K/120K miss headline, you're missing where the actual restriction is landing.
items.tweetB: Small caps are having their best H1 since 1991 in the stock market. Small-business hiring is still contracting y/y in the labor market. Two completely different 'small business' economies -- one trades on Robinhood, one signs paychecks. Don't confuse them.

charts.desc: Chart of Bitcoin/generic market image attached to the chat post

items.title: Trump's 2025 disclosure: $1B+ in crypto income, including $635M off a memecoin now down 98%
items.idea: Trump's OGE financial disclosure shows over $1.2B in 2025 crypto earnings: $635M in TRUMP memecoin royalties, $500M+ in net proceeds from World Liberty Financial token sales, plus $50M+ each in BTC and ETH, and a stablecoin holding-company stake reportedly tied to an Abu Dhabi investment generating $196M in revenue. The TRUMP token itself is down ~98% from its $75 all-time high, now a $394M market cap.
items.angle: Royalty income is structured off issuance, not price, so the president collected nine figures while the token that generated it collapsed 98% -- a template for how these arrangements are built to extract value regardless of what happens to holders.
items.tweetA: Trump's memecoin is down 98% from its ATH and he still collected $635M in royalties off it. The token cratering didn't touch his income at all because royalties are structured off issuance, not price. That's the tell on how these arrangements are built to extract value regardless of holder outcomes.
items.tweetB: $1.2B in personal crypto income while your administration writes crypto market structure law. Congress is arguing whether to add an ethics clause to CLARITY. That debate should have been over the day this disclosure dropped, not the reason the bill's odds fell 10 points.

charts.desc: Chart of Trump's crypto asset breakdown embedded in the newsletter

items.title: 140 companies including BlackRock and Coinbase just launched a stablecoin that gives Circle's yield back to partners
items.idea: 140+ companies -- Coinbase, Visa, Stripe, Mastercard, BlackRock, BNY, Google, Shopify -- launched Open USD, a stablecoin where businesses mint/redeem for free and partners receive nearly all reserve earnings instead of the issuer keeping them. Open Standard is led by Zach Abrams (Bridge co-founder, sold to Stripe for $1.1B). Circle (CRCL) fell 13-18% on the news, hitting its lowest price since late February; Coinbase slipped ~4% despite signing on to the new coalition.
items.angle: This is the stablecoin market's Amazon Basics moment -- infrastructure players just realized they don't need to pay Circle's toll, and Circle's entire bull case (rising business/institutional adoption) is now structurally at risk from its own future customers.
items.tweetA: Circle's whole bull case was 'more businesses will hold USDC.' Now 140 of those exact businesses just built a stablecoin that gives THEM the yield instead of Circle. The rising-tide thesis just built its own boat. $CRCL down 13-18% and that's probably not the bottom of the repricing.
items.tweetB: Reserve yield on a stablecoin is the entire business. Circle keeps almost all of it today. Open USD gives it back to partners minus a small fee. That's the difference between a toll booth and a co-op -- and Stripe, Visa, BlackRock just chose the co-op. $CRCL $COIN

charts.desc: Chart of crypto majors' daily price moves embedded in the newsletter

items.title: SEC opens a 60-day comment period that could bring prediction markets into the ETF wrapper
items.idea: The SEC opened a 60-day public comment period (27 questions) on whether 'novel' ETFs built around crypto and event contracts should be classified as investment companies under the Investment Company Act -- after clearing BTC/ETH/SOL/DOGE funds while blocking roughly two dozen prediction-market ETF filings. TD Cowen's Jaret Seiberg expects the SEC is laying groundwork for a broader rule covering event-contract and single-stock strategies, potentially arriving as soon as 2027.
items.angle: This is the SEC quietly building the legal category needed to bring Kalshi/Polymarket-style prediction markets into a regulated fund wrapper -- a multi-year process, but the direction of travel matters for anyone positioned in prediction-market infrastructure.
items.tweetA: Why did the SEC freeze two dozen prediction-market ETF filings while approving Dogecoin funds? Because they don't have a legal category for event contracts yet. The 60-day comment period that just opened is them building one -- TD Cowen sees a rule by 2027.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the Kalshi-Michigan gambling fight play out in state court. The actual prediction-market regulatory story is a quiet 27-question SEC comment letter nobody's tweeting about. That's where the real market-structure decision gets made.

items.title: Citi cuts its 12-month Bitcoin target 27%, from $112K to $82K
items.idea: Citi cut its 12-month Bitcoin price target from $112K to $82K and its ETH target from $3,175 to $2,240, citing ETF outflows, slow regulatory progress, and digital-asset-treasury (DAT) concerns. Separately, TD Cowen cut Strategy's price target by 35% citing the new framework that opens the door to Strategy selling Bitcoin. BTC sits near $58.6K, and CLARITY Act passage odds fell 10 points to 39% on the Trump crypto disclosure news.
items.angle: A sell-side target cut of this magnitude from a major bank, paired with BTC's first close below its 200-week moving average since 2023, is the kind of capitulation-adjacent signal that historically marks late-cycle bear conditions rather than early ones.
items.tweetA: Citi just cut its BTC target 27% in one note -- $112K to $82K. That's not a gradual re-rate, that's capitulation math from a sell-side desk. Combine it with the first sub-200-week-MA close since 2023 and you have two independent signals pointing the same direction at once.
items.tweetB: Everyone citing the BTC 200-week MA break as bearish confirmation is using the same indicator that flagged generational buying opportunities in 2018 and 2022. The question isn't whether the signal is bearish right now -- it obviously is -- it's whether it's early or late in this cycle.

items.title: Mag 7 shed $2.3T in June while the Russell 2000 had its best H1 since 1991 -- the AI trade migrated, it didn't die
items.idea: Mag 7 lost $2.3 trillion in June. In the same window the Russell 2000 posted its best H1 since 1991, +21% YTD versus the S&P's +7.5%. Sixteen of the Russell's top 50 performers are small-cap semis -- AEHR, ICHR, MXL, all up 400%+. Dauber's read: the market isn't abandoning AI, it's repricing who gets paid, rotating down-cap to the second- and third-derivative plays nobody's watching because everyone's staring at the Mag 7 body count.
items.angle: This is a rotation story hiding in plain sight -- institutional flow chasing Mag7 headlines is missing where the actual AI capex is landing in equity form.
items.tweetA: Everyone's doing a Mag7 body count -- $2.3T lost in June. Meanwhile the Russell 2000 just had its best H1 since 1991, and 16 of its top 50 names are small-cap semis up 400%+. The AI trade isn't dying, it's rotating to names nobody's watching.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this: $650B in Big Tech capex this year, and the equity market is telling you the money isn't landing where the headlines say. AEHR, ICHR, MXL up 400%+ while AAPL and MSFT are red on green days. That's real distribution, not noise.

charts.desc: Charts inside -- worth opening (paywalled beyond intro)

items.title: There's no equity-friendly outcome from Warsh's Sintra appearance today
items.idea: Fed funds swaps price roughly 36% odds of a July hike. Warsh appears on a panel with Lagarde, Bailey, and Macklem at Sintra today -- the real test of whether markets believe him. Fed's Hammack already said this week she's 'not seeing a lot of restraint' in the economy and flagged AI infrastructure spending as a persistent inflation driver. Dauber's framing: confirming the hawkish tone sends the 30Y toward 5%; softening it means admitting the last 175bp of cuts were a mistake.
items.angle: This is a genuine two-sided risk event today, not a formality -- worth sizing volatility around the Sintra appearance specifically rather than waiting for the July 29 FOMC.
items.tweetA: 36% odds of a July hike priced into swaps. Warsh has to either confirm that and send the 30Y toward 5%, or walk it back and admit the last 175bp of cuts were a mistake. Read that twice -- there is genuinely no equity-friendly outcome from today's Sintra panel.
items.tweetB: MOVE index at 68.14 -- historically low, bond vol priced for nothing to happen. Warsh speaks in hours, NFP in 24. That's either correctly boring or the cheapest tail hedge in the market right now. I know which side of that bet I'm on.

items.title: SCOTUS quietly ended 90 years of independent-agency protection this week, and markets shrugged
items.idea: SCOTUS overruled Humphrey's Executor in Trump v. Slaughter, ending 90 years of precedent shielding heads of independent agencies (FTC, and by extension every other 'independent' executive agency) from at-will presidential removal. Dauber notes markets treated it as a Tuesday, but flags the obvious follow-on question: if the FTC chair can be fired at will today, why not eventually the Fed?
items.angle: This is the legal precedent that made Fed independence structurally possible -- the market shrugging it off today doesn't mean it stays irrelevant the first time a Fed chair's stance conflicts with the White House.
items.tweetA: SCOTUS just killed 90 years of precedent protecting independent agency heads from being fired at will, and equities treated it as a Tuesday. Nobody's pricing that this is the legal foundation that made 'the Fed can't be fired' a settled question. It just became a lot less settled.
items.tweetB: If the president can now fire the FTC chair at will, what's actually stopping a future test case at the Fed? The market answer today was 'nothing to see here.' I'd bet that repricing happens fast and ugly the first time it's tested, not gradually.

items.title: Anthropic's export ban got lifted 19 days after it was imposed, in exchange for a safety-cooperation pledge
items.idea: The Trump administration lifted export controls on Fable 5 and Mythos 5 nineteen days after issuing them, after Anthropic 'pledged closer cooperation with the US government on safety testing.' Dauber's framing: frontier AI guardrails are now demonstrably negotiable political rents, one at a time, alongside this week's Fed politicization and the SCOTUS ruling on independent agencies.
items.angle: This is a template, not a one-off -- expect every frontier lab to now treat export policy as a negotiable variable contingent on government cooperation commitments, which changes the calculus on regulatory-driven model release timing.
items.tweetA: Nineteen days. That's how long an export ban on frontier AI models lasted before it got traded away for a 'closer cooperation on safety testing' pledge. If export controls can be negotiated away in three weeks, they were never really controls -- they were leverage.
items.tweetB: Same week SCOTUS guts independent-agency protections and the Fed chair does his hawkish-dove balancing act at Sintra, a frontier AI export ban gets reversed for a cooperation pledge. Every institutional guardrail this week turned out to be negotiable. That's the actual story of the week, not any single headline.

items.title: Yen at 162.46, weakest since 1986 -- $73B of intervention didn't move it
items.idea: The yen sits at 162.46, its weakest since 1986. Japan spent $73B on intervention through May with no lasting effect -- BOJ at 1.00% versus the Fed at 3.50-3.75%, a 275bp gap the currency alone can't close. Scotiabank flags 162 as the intervention trigger zone. Dauber's mechanism: if Japan intervenes at scale by selling Treasuries to defend the level, it hits UST yields during the exact window Warsh speaks and NFP prints, unwinding carry trades simultaneously.
items.angle: Yen is the bridge asset connecting rates, equities, and FX right now -- a genuinely underpriced tail risk given how much carry-trade leverage sits on top of it.
items.tweetA: $73B in yen intervention through May. Currency is still at a 40-year low. That's not a warning shot that failed, that's proof the 275bp BOJ-Fed rate gap makes intervention arithmetic pointless without an actual policy change on one side or the other.
items.tweetB: Nobody's pricing the yen as a US rates risk, but if Japan intervenes by selling Treasuries to defend 162, that hits UST yields during the exact week Warsh speaks and NFP prints. The carry trade unwind risk isn't a Japan story, it's a global duration story wearing a yen costume.

items.title: Margin debt hit a record $1.42T, +53.7% y/y, while hedge funds quietly de-risked into the rally
items.idea: Margin debt hit a record $1.42T, +53.7% year-on-year, +8.5% month-on-month (FINRA, May) -- the best H1 for stocks in 35 years is also the most leveraged H1 in history. Meanwhile long-only funds poured a record ~$180B into equities in Q2 while hedge funds and CTAs cut exposure into the same tape, and quant momentum funds posted their worst rout of the year as momentum strategies flipped.
items.angle: Passive/long-only money is now the marginal buyer at record leverage while smart-money proxies (HF, CTA, quant momentum) are de-risking -- a classic late-cycle positioning mismatch that makes any correction mechanically self-reinforcing.
items.tweetA: Margin debt +53.7% y/y to a record $1.42T. Best H1 for stocks in 35 years is also the most leveraged H1 ever recorded. Those two facts aren't independent -- the leverage is a big part of why the H1 was so good, which is exactly the problem on the way down.
items.tweetB: Record $180B in long-only inflows this quarter while hedge funds and CTAs cut exposure into the same tape. Passive money is now the marginal buyer while professional risk-takers head for the exits. That's not consensus bullishness -- that's one cohort left holding the tape.

items.title: Taiwan holds 11 days of LNG reserves, 97% of its energy is imported -- and China could choke it without firing a shot
items.idea: 97% of Taiwan's energy is imported, the highest rate of any major economy. It holds roughly 11 days of LNG reserves and 42 days of coal. CSIS wargaming: once coal reserves are exhausted in a blockade scenario, Taiwan's total electricity production falls to about 20% of pre-blockade levels and manufacturing ceases. China could achieve this by blockading just three LNG terminals, without firing a shot -- simply requiring permits for vessels in what it deems its territorial waters. TSMC alone consumes 8-10% of Taiwan's grid.
items.angle: This is a policy failure two decades in the making (nuclear phase-out without adequate renewable buildout, only ~12% renewables vs a 20% target) -- any serious escalation scenario is as much an energy-security story as a military one.
items.tweetA: 11 days of LNG. 42 days of coal. That's Taiwan's entire strategic energy buffer, and TSMC alone eats 8-10% of the grid on a normal day. China doesn't need a shooting war to cripple this -- blockading 3 LNG terminals gets there. The chip supply chain risk everyone models is downstream of an energy story nobody prices.
items.tweetB: Taiwan killed its nuclear program for ideological reasons, then missed its renewable target (12% actual vs 20% goal), then discovered China's own coal-plant demand destruction is the only reason it isn't already in an energy crisis. 'Thank Beijing for the LNG cargoes' is not the sentence you want your energy security policy summarized as.

charts.desc: Podcast/radio-show format -- video available on YouTube

items.title: 8% of World Cup Round-of-32 players have Balkan heritage -- a region that's 0.2% of the world's population
items.idea: 99 World Cup players were born in France; 76 of them represent countries other than France (Senegal alone has ten French-born players). Separately, players with Western Balkans heritage make up roughly 8% of Round-of-32 rosters despite the region being about 0.2% of world population -- a far bigger overrepresentation multiple than Latin America (8% of players, 17% of world population) or Africa (17% of players, 17% of world population) get relative to their population share.
items.angle: A striking case study in how colonial migration and small, high-churn diaspora populations compound into elite output decades later -- a pattern-recognition template that extends well beyond football.
items.tweetA: 0.2% of the world's population. 8% of the Round of 32 World Cup rosters. That's the Balkans-heritage overrepresentation at this tournament -- a bigger multiple than Latin America or Africa get relative to their population share. Small, high-churn diaspora populations keep punching absurdly above weight in elite output, and it's not just football.
items.tweetB: 76 France-born players are on OTHER countries' World Cup squads right now. That's not a football stat, that's 60 years of French colonial migration showing up as a labor market outcome two generations later. Every 'sudden' demographic story has a decades-old root cause hiding in plain sight.

items.title: Bitcoin closes below its 200-week moving average for the first time since 2023
items.idea: BTC closed below its 200-week moving average for the first time since 2023, alongside the worst monthly Bitcoin ETF outflow on record and a Fear & Greed reading of 10 (extreme fear). Wintermute called current conditions a 'late-stage bear market.' Meanwhile Bitmine added 27K ETH and SharpLink bought 10K more, and 84% of Binance altcoins now sit below their 200-day average.
items.angle: Multiple independent bearish signals (technical MA break, record ETF outflow, extreme-fear sentiment index, sell-side target cuts) are aligning simultaneously -- either genuine confirmation or a crowded trade due for a snapback.
items.tweetA: BTC below its 200-week moving average for the first time since 2023. Fear & Greed at 10. Worst ETF outflow month on record. Every signal is screaming the same thing at the same time -- which is exactly when I get suspicious rather than confident.
items.tweetB: When was the last time BTC closed below the 200-week MA and it turned out to be the buy signal instead of the sell signal? 2018 and 2022 both say check your history before you short the fear.

charts.desc: Link aggregator format -- no embedded charts

items.source: The Bond Beat (citing BCA Research / Arthur Budaghyan)
items.title: US tech capex just passed the peak intensity of the 2000 dot-com bubble, measured against GDP
items.idea: BCA Research's Arthur Budaghyan: housing bubbles historically burst once residential investment hit 7% of GDP in the US, 9% in Japan, 8% in Spain -- and halved swiftly after. US tech capex peaked at 5% of GDP (nominal) in 2000; in real terms today's tech capex is already above 7% of GDP. Meanwhile capex on everything OTHER than tech is actually falling, and non-tech imports are declining while tech imports keep rising.
items.angle: This is the single most concrete, falsifiable 'AI capex bubble' data point in the window -- a direct historical comparison to housing bubble peaks from a credible independent research shop, not a hot take.
items.tweetA: Tech capex is now above 7% of US GDP in real terms. Every major housing bubble in history (US, Japan, Spain) burst somewhere between 7-9% of GDP and halved within a few years after. BCA Research just put the AI capex boom on the exact same chart as 2008, and the x-axis lines up.
items.tweetB: Non-tech capex is falling while tech capex rises. Non-tech imports are falling while tech imports rise. That's not an AI boom lifting the whole economy -- that's AI capex crowding out everything else in the economy that isn't AI. Different thesis than 'AI is additive to GDP.'

charts.desc: Bloomberg chart: tech capex as % of GDP vs historical housing bubble peaks

items.source: The Bond Beat (citing Challenger, Gray & Christmas)
items.title: June layoffs fell 53%, but AI has now been the top cited reason for four straight months
items.idea: Challenger, Gray & Christmas: June layoffs cooled to 45,849, down 53% from May. AI has been cited as the top reason for layoffs for the fourth consecutive month. CNBC separately reported that employers who laid off workers citing AI are already starting to regret those decisions.
items.angle: Falling layoff volume doesn't mean the AI-labor story is over -- it's now the dominant stated reason even as volume drops, and the 'employer regret' angle suggests a second-order re-hiring wave nobody's modeling into labor cost assumptions yet.
items.tweetA: AI has been the #1 stated reason for layoffs for 4 straight months, and now employers are quietly starting to regret those cuts. Nobody's pricing the re-hiring wave that follows a bad AI-driven layoff decision -- that's a labor cost story hiding behind a headline about layoffs falling 53%.
items.tweetB: 45,849 layoffs in June, down 53% from May. Sounds like good news for the labor market. It's also the 4th straight month where AI was the top cited reason. Fewer layoffs doesn't mean the AI substitution story stopped -- it might just mean the easy cuts are already done.

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: Air Products took a $2.9B charge to kill a hydrogen megaproject -- and the stock rose 8%
items.idea: Air Products canceled its Louisiana Clean Energy Complex and other hydrogen projects, taking up to $2.9B in pre-tax charges ($2.2B after-tax), while redeploying capital toward higher-return NEOM ammonia exports and its core industrial gas business. The stock rose 8% on the news -- the market rewarded exiting a stranded clean-energy megaproject over completing it.
items.angle: A real-time data point on 'return-on-capital discipline beats decarbonization ambition' playing out in industrial capex decisions right now -- worth watching whether other hydrogen-for-mobility bets get similarly written down through 2026-27.
items.tweetA: Air Products just took a $2.9B charge to KILL a hydrogen project and the stock went up 8%. The market is telling you clearly: exiting a stranded decarbonization megaproject is worth more than finishing it. That's a signal for every other hydrogen-for-mobility bet still on corporate balance sheets.
items.tweetB: $2.9B pre-tax charge, stock up 8% same day. Investors are pricing the removal of multi-year execution risk as worth more than the sunk cost of the project itself. If you're holding other hydrogen-mobility names, ask whether they're due for the same math. $APD

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Oil stopped panicking because China and the IEA drew down reserves -- now those barrels have to go back
items.idea: Hormuz running at roughly 70% of normal traffic capacity is proving sufficient to avoid a full crisis -- tankers rerouted, pipelines absorbing part of the load, refiners adjusting runs. China's crude stockpile drawdown (not fresh buying) and IEA emergency releases quietly absorbed the disruption. The author's thesis: the next oil bid may come from the restocking cycle as China, the IEA, and India rebuild the reserves they just drew down -- a quiet, non-headline-driven demand shift rather than a fresh escalation.
items.angle: The market is pricing the crisis as over because the physical system coped, but coping isn't the same as well-supplied -- the reserve rebuild cycle is a slower, less headline-driven bid that could catch a complacent market off guard in H2.
items.tweetA: Oil stopped panicking because China and the IEA drew down strategic reserves to plug the Hormuz gap. Everyone's reading that as 'crisis over.' Nobody's pricing that those barrels have to go back into storage eventually -- and refilling a strategic reserve is a very different kind of demand than the market is modeling.
items.tweetB: What happens to oil when China decides its stockpile cushion just proved too valuable to run this low again? That's not a geopolitical escalation story, it's a boring inventory-rebuild story, and boring stories are exactly the kind the market misses until the barrels are already gone.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Markets are pricing 45bp of Fed tightening by 2027 -- and Warsh doesn't need to say much to confirm it
items.idea: Markets have roughly 45bp of Fed tightening priced by Q2 2027, with 22bp for September and 8bp already priced for the July 29 meeting. DXY is holding 101.00 support, with 101.75-102 back in play on a hawkish Sintra performance plus firm ADP/ISM data. EUR/USD stays vulnerable below 1.1350 given softer eurozone inflation against a more credible hawkish Fed.
items.angle: The tactical FX version of the broader Fed-credibility story -- useful for sizing the DXY/EURUSD trade specifically around today's Sintra event risk rather than waiting for the July 29 FOMC.
items.tweetA: 45bp of hikes priced by 2027, with the market already assigning 8bp of probability to a July 29 hike. That's not 'the Fed might hike' -- that's the market quietly pricing real odds of tightening into an economy the Fed itself just called an AI-capex inflation risk. Warsh doesn't need to say much to confirm it.
items.tweetB: DXY sitting right on 101 support going into Sintra. I don't think Warsh needs a single hawkish surprise today -- he just needs to not walk back what markets already believe, and 101.75-102 is back in play by Friday.

items.title: Top VC funds run private Claude/LLM triage on every inbound deck before a human sees it
items.idea: Sequoia, Accel, and Andreessen Horowitz run private LLM/Claude API integrations to auto-tag inbound decks for sector, stage, and red flags before a human ever sees them. Notable Capital uses Claude with MCP integrations to manage 500+ inbound intros a year with a two-person team. The extraction layer cross-references deck claims against the company's own public materials and flags contradictions automatically -- one cited case flagged a robotics startup whose deck described 'pilot stage' while its own website claimed active commercial deployments.
items.angle: A verifiable, specific data point on AI eating the venture pipeline function itself -- worth tracking as a leading indicator for how fast AI-native workflows are compressing headcount in white-collar screening and diligence roles generally, not just VC.
items.tweetA: A two-person team managing 500+ inbound VC intros a year via Claude MCP integrations. That's 250 deals per person, per year, on triage alone. Compare that to the analyst headcount a fund needed for that volume three years ago and you have the actual AI-jobs story, not the hypothetical one.
items.tweetB: Everyone debates whether AI will replace analysts. Sequoia, Accel, and a16z already run LLM triage on every inbound deck before a human sees it, cross-referencing your deck against your own website for contradictions in real time. The debate is over at the top of the funnel -- it's already been automated.

items.title: 'Coins left exchanges' is not automatically bullish -- the same flow means opposite things depending on regime
items.idea: Adler's framework: exchange outflows/inflows are not inherently directional signals -- the same coin movement means opposite things depending on market regime (strength vs stress) and sender identity (whale vs retail, spot vs derivatives). Confident accumulation and panic self-custody withdrawal produce the identical on-chain arrow. BTC flow (supply side) and stablecoin inflows (demand side) should never be merged into one 'liquidity' metric -- flow confirms, it doesn't predict.
items.angle: A useful corrective to the 'exchange outflows = bullish' narrative implicit in several other newsletters this morning reporting raw flow data as directional signal without this context layer.
items.tweetA: Half the crypto newsletters this morning report 'coins left exchanges' like it's automatically bullish. It isn't. Confident accumulation and panic self-custody withdrawal produce the exact same on-chain arrow. If your flow analysis doesn't separate regime from direction, you're reading noise as signal.
items.tweetB: BTC flow is supply. Stablecoin flow is demand. Merging them into one 'liquidity' number is like averaging a company's revenue and its debt and calling it 'financial health.' Different axis, different question, and most on-chain dashboards still get this wrong.

charts.desc: Paywalled beyond the intro -- Flow Context Matrix graphic likely inside

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (citing Goldman Sachs)
items.title: Goldman flags a developing super El Niño as a 2027 food-cost risk, not a 2026 one
items.idea: Goldman Sachs' European food research on a developing 'super El Niño': not yet a 2026 earnings event, but a growing threat to 2027 food costs. Cocoa remains the clearest pressure point, with almonds, barley, oats, palm, and oilseeds potentially joining. Relative winners: water and ice cream demand; relative losers: chocolate, which faces higher inputs and a weaker consumer backdrop at the same time.
items.angle: An 18-month-lead-time trade idea worth tagging now (cocoa futures, almond/edible-oil exposure, ice cream/water beverage names) while it's still off most desks' radar.
items.tweetA: What does a super El Niño actually do to your portfolio? Goldman's answer: not much in 2026, a lot by 2027 -- cocoa first, then almonds, barley, oats, palm oil. This is the kind of 18-month-lead-time trade that's boring right now and won't be boring in Q1 2027.
items.tweetB: Either El Niño intensifies and cocoa/almonds/oilseeds see a real supply shock in 2027, or it doesn't and this is a non-event. The asymmetry is that positioning for it now costs almost nothing because nobody's pricing it, and the downside of being wrong is just opportunity cost.

items.title: Alan Greenspan has died -- the same week Warsh is dismantling the exact playbook Greenspan built
items.idea: Alan Greenspan has died. Paper Alfa's framing: Greenspan fundamentally changed the relationship between central banks and markets by building a career on telegraphing intentions (the 'Greenspan put' era). Kevin Warsh is now attempting to redefine that relationship again -- specifically by moving away from forward guidance entirely, which the piece calls potentially the most significant shift in central banking since Greenspan's tenure.
items.angle: A genuine historical-parallel framing device for everything else in this window (Warsh at Sintra, the Fed credibility bind, the SCOTUS independent-agency ruling) -- worth using as the connective thread across today's Fed stories.
items.tweetA: Alan Greenspan died the same week Kevin Warsh is at Sintra trying to prove the Fed doesn't do forward guidance anymore. Greenspan built modern central banking on telegraphing intentions to markets. Warsh is dismantling that exact playbook in real time. That's not a coincidence worth ignoring.
items.tweetB: The 'Greenspan put' assumed markets could always read the Fed's next move. Warsh's entire strategy is refusing to give you that read. If that holds, every Fed-day options strategy built over the last 30 years needs to be rethought, not just adjusted.
